coredata.dc:title: Multi-purpose nitrogen and phosphorous codoped carbon nanodots for fluorescence-based sensing and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Chatzimitakos, Theodoros G.
Kasouni, Athanasia I.
Stalikas, Constantine D.
coredata.dc:description:
A single-step, straightforward hydrothermal method is put forward to synthesize multi-purpose nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped carbon nanodots (N,P-CNDs). Endowed with excellent water-solubility, chemical, and optical stability, the synthesized CΝDs showed high prospects for utilization in chemical and physicochemical probing and bioimaging. Specifically, the CNDs were successfully applied as a pH and benzophenone-1 probe with satisfactory precision and accuracy, as well as to assess the activity of urease. The fluorescence intensity of CNDs decreases linearly with the pH rising within the range of 3.0–12.0. Based on the benzophenone-induced fluorescence quenching of the CNDs, an acceptable linear regression for detecting benzophenone-1 was achieved over the concentration range of 1.7–76 μM. Furthermore, the synthesized CNDs demonstrated exceptional potential for cell imaging, especially by selectively labeling cell nuclei. Capitalizing on the bioimaging capabilities and pH-sensing properties of the CNDs, we developed an integrated system for intracellular pH monitoring, which can enable improved understanding of cellular processes. This versatile system opens up new opportunities for the application of CNDs in sensing various chemical and physicochemical parameters, thereby expanding the horizons of their utilization in diverse fields. The fact that the synthesized CNDs exhibit all the above capabilities enables the development of a multi-purpose probing system. The findings of this study contribute to the expanding body of knowledge of CNDs applications, whereas the CNDs can be used in diverse fields, such as environmental monitoring and analysis, as well as in biological and medical research.
Environmental implication To date, there have been reports about the endocrine-disrupting properties of benzophenones and their bioaccumulation in human tissues, genotoxicity, etc. Despite the importance in their detection, the number of analytical methods developed for the analysis of benzophenones is limited. In this study, the development of a fluorescence probe for the sensitive and selective determination of benzophenone-1 is described. Moreover, the developed carbon dots could also serve as a pH probe. This property was utilized for monitoring cellular pH changes, which may alter due to environmental conditions. Benzophenones have raised concerns due to their reported endocrine-disrupting properties, bioaccumulation in human tissues, and genotoxicity. Addressing the gap of limited analytical methods, our study focuses on the development of a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence probe for the determination of benzophenone-1. By successfully synthesizing carbon dots with unique optical properties, we not only enable the detection of benzophenone-1 but also harness the pH-sensing capabilities of these carbon dots. This dual functionality allows the monitoring of cellular pH changes, which can be influenced by environmental conditions. Through our research, we provide a valuable tool for the detection of benzophenones and pH monitoring, thereby supporting efforts towards environmental monitoring and risk assessment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped carbon nanodots
Benzophenone-1
pH sensing
Intracellular pH sensing
Enzyme activity

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability and selectivity study of the CNDs
Detection of urea/urease activity based on the pH response of N,P-CNDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors want to thank the Laboratory of Immunology and Laboratory of Zoology of the Biological Applications and Technology Department (University of Ioannina) for providing access to their facilities. Many thanks are also due to Dr. L. Sygellou and Prof. A. Avgeropoulos for obtaining XPS spectra and TEM images, respectively. Finally, thanks are also due to I. Chousidis for his assistance in obtaining the fluorescence microscopy images.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectrum of the synthesized N,P-CNDS.
TEM image (right) and size distribution (left) of the synthesized CNDs.
Deconvoluted C1s (A), N1s (B) and P2p (c) XPS spectra of the synthesized CNDs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of an aqueous solution of N,P-CNDs, under various excitation wavelengths.
Linear response of the N,P-CNDs PL intensity to the change of the pH.
Fluorescence microscopy images (magnification 40x) of HaCaT cells treated with N,P-CNDs at pH 3.0 (A), 4.0 (B), 5.0 (C), 6.0 (D) and 7.0 (E).
PL intensity of HaCaT cells treated with N,P-CNDs, at various pH values.
Stern-Volmer plot of N,P-CNDs relative PL quenching vs the concentration of BP1.
Measurement of the pH of different samples with a pH-meter and the proposed CNDs-based fluorescence probe.

head.ce:title: Multi-purpose nitrogen and phosphorous codoped carbon nanodots for fluorescence-based sensing and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A single-step, straightforward hydrothermal method is put forward to synthesize multi-purpose nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped carbon nanodots (N,P-CNDs). Endowed with excellent water-solubility, chemical, and optical stability, the synthesized CΝDs showed high prospects for utilization in chemical and physicochemical probing and bioimaging. Specifically, the CNDs were successfully applied as a pH and benzophenone-1 probe with satisfactory precision and accuracy, as well as to assess the activity of urease. The fluorescence intensity of CNDs decreases linearly with the pH rising within the range of 3.0–12.0. Based on the benzophenone-induced fluorescence quenching of the CNDs, an acceptable linear regression for detecting benzophenone-1 was achieved over the concentration range of 1.7–76 μM. Furthermore, the synthesized CNDs demonstrated exceptional potential for cell imaging, especially by selectively labeling cell nuclei. Capitalizing on the bioimaging capabilities and pH-sensing properties of the CNDs, we developed an integrated system for intracellular pH monitoring, which can enable improved understanding of cellular processes. This versatile system opens up new opportunities for the application of CNDs in sensing various chemical and physicochemical parameters, thereby expanding the horizons of their utilization in diverse fields. The fact that the synthesized CNDs exhibit all the above capabilities enables the development of a multi-purpose probing system. The findings of this study contribute to the expanding body of knowledge of CNDs applications, whereas the CNDs can be used in diverse fields, such as environmental monitoring and analysis, as well as in biological and medical research.

To date, there have been reports about the endocrine-disrupting properties of benzophenones and their bioaccumulation in human tissues, genotoxicity, etc. Despite the importance in their detection, the number of analytical methods developed for the analysis of benzophenones is limited. In this study, the development of a fluorescence probe for the sensitive and selective determination of benzophenone-1 is described. Moreover, the developed carbon dots could also serve as a pH probe. This property was utilized for monitoring cellular pH changes, which may alter due to environmental conditions.

Benzophenones have raised concerns due to their reported endocrine-disrupting properties, bioaccumulation in human tissues, and genotoxicity. Addressing the gap of limited analytical methods, our study focuses on the development of a highly sensitive and selective fluorescence probe for the determination of benzophenone-1. By successfully synthesizing carbon dots with unique optical properties, we not only enable the detection of benzophenone-1 but also harness the pH-sensing capabilities of these carbon dots. This dual functionality allows the monitoring of cellular pH changes, which can be influenced by environmental conditions. Through our research, we provide a valuable tool for the detection of benzophenones and pH monitoring, thereby supporting efforts towards environmental monitoring and risk assessment.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped carbon nanodots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) have emerged as a valuable class of fluorescent nanoparticles that exhibit biocompatibility, resistance to photobleaching, and diversity in composition and characteristics among the different types. These newly developed nano-fluorescent probes have garnered significant attention to such an extent, that nowadays it has been established as a standalone research field. Their photoluminescence (PL) properties have been reaped in numerous applications, such as optoelectronics, cell imaging, (bio)sensing of small molecules, etc. [1,2]. Numerous articles have focused on the synthesis of CNDs from various precursor materials and new applications of CNDs. To this end, various CNDs have been developed based on chemical or physical methods, such as solvothermal, chemical oxidation, electrochemical treatment, and carbonization/pyrolysis of various defined chemicals or natural products (e.g. citric acid, urea, glucose, graphite, orange and lemon peels, apple seeds, milk, honey, roses, human fingernails, etc.) [2–11]. Compared with pure CNDs, heteroatom doping can further increase the quantum yield and endow them with various new or improved structural, optical and physicochemical properties [12,13]. Nitrogen is commonly used for doping of CNDS, since it has five valence electrons and exhibits structural similarity to carbon [14]. However, despite its abundance, compared to other elements that can be used for CNDs doping, and bestow different properties on CNDs, nitrogen doping fails to improve significantly the quantum yield (QY) of the CNDs [15]. To this end, doping with phosphorus, an electron-rich element that can serve as a donor, was found to be effective in improving the fluorescence properties of the CNDs [14]. As such, doping with both elements is gaining ground in the field of CNDs synthesis. For instance, Yu et al. synthesized N,P-CNDs via a hydrothermal approach by heating a mixture of m-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid in an autoclave at 220 °C for 24 h and the resulting CNDs were found to have a QY of 67.53% [16]. Likewise, N,P-CNDs with a QY of 59% were synthesized from the hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and diammonium hydrogen phosphate at 180 °C, for 4 h [15]. Based on the above, it is evident that the combination of nitrogen and phosphorus may be a promising combination of dopants, so as to obtain CNDs with high QY.

Benzophenone-1 (BZ1) is extensively used in the cosmetics industry. This is due to its UV-absorbing properties that help protect personal care products from deterioration or discoloration. However, mounting evidence implicate the use of BZ1 in the development of cancer, such as breast cancer, ovarian cancer, etc. [17–19]. Furthermore, benzophenones have been associated with endocrine-disrupting properties, bioaccumulation in human tissues, genotoxicity, and other health concerns [18,20,21]. The International Agency for Research on Cancer has classified benzophenones as potentially carcinogenic to humans [22]. Due to the above, there is much controversy over its use in cosmetics and personal care products. Unfortunately, the number of analytical methods developed for the analysis of benzophenones is limited [22,23]. Their low concentrations in environmental samples necessitates high selectivity and sensitivity. Therefore, the use of more sophisticated techniques, such as HPLC-MS/MS have been proposed, albeit at significantly higher analysis costs [24]. To the best of our knowledge, despite the numerous CND-based luminescence systems, none of them have been developed for the sensing of BZ1, until now. Thus, the development of a CND-based sensing system for BZ1 represents a notable and unprecedented advancement in the field.

The development of CNDs through cost-effective and convenient synthesis methods holds significant importance for expanding their utility in diverse applications. Moreover, most reported luminescent CNDs are limited to very specific applications, however it is highly essential to build CNDs for multi-purpose novel applications [25]. Among these applications, pH stands out as a critical parameter in various industries and research fields. In particular, intracellular pH plays a pivotal role in cellular processes associated with cellular behaviors and pathological conditions [26], including cell growth and apoptosis, receptor-mediated signal transduction, etc. Maintaining pH homeostasis is vital for cellular functions, given that most proteins exhibit distinct pH-dependent activity ranges. Additionally, the acidic environment of lysosomes (pH 4.5–5.5) facilitates protein degradation in cellular metabolism [27]. Finally, numerous metabolic pathways require stringent regulation of the pH. Although glass-pH-electrodes have excellent performance, they have some drawbacks, including rigid design and fragility, and limited suitability for small volume samples or intracellular pH measurements in biological applications. As such, there has been an increasing interest in using nanomaterials as pH sensors. However, their limited pH range as well as the low dispersibility of many nanomaterials in aqueous solutions pose challenges to their applicability [28]. Hence, the development of CNDs as pH sensors holds promise due to their unique properties and advantages. CNDs offer the potential for cost-effective synthesis, tunable pH response, excellent dispersibility in aqueous solutions, and compatibility with biological systems. In fact, more and more studies are being published, in an effort to develop CNDs that can be used as pH sensors, substantiating the importance of the field, especially in biochemical systems [29–32].

The design and synthesis of multi-purpose CNDs capable of reliable sensing of multiple targets holds immense potential for practical applications in chemical analysis and biomedicine. In this study, we present a microwave-based synthesis of CNDs from 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone and phosphoric acid towards yielding nitrogen and phosphorous co-doped CNDs. The fabricated N,P-CNDs exhibit multifunctionality, serving as fluorescent probes for the detection of benzophenone-1 (BZ1) in water and pH measurement. In the same context, the CND properties were, also, capitalized on for assessing the activity of urease. In addition to the above, the proposed N,P-CNDs exhibit remarkable capabilities for cell imaging, specifically for the selective labeling of the cell nucleus. Notably, the pH-sensing and bioimaging properties of the CNDs were successfully combined to develop a fluorescence imaging-based system for sensing intracellular pH alterations. Overall, our study showcases the potential of these CNDs as a versatile platform for various sensing applications, particularly in the fields of chemical analysis and biomedicine.
All details about the chemicals used in this study are given in detail in Supplementary Material.
All information about the instruments used in this study is given in detail in Supplementary Material.

In a glass beaker, 1 mL of conc. phosphoric acid was mixed with 1 mL of 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone. The mixture was microwaved in a domestic microwave oven at 350 Watt, for 2 min and the produced viscous brown liquid was diluted with distilled water. Then, the pH was neutralized with NaOH, and the solution was dialyzed (dialysis membrane MWCO: 2000) against water until no sodium and phosphate ions had been detected in the dialysate. Next, the solution was collected, freeze-dried, and the obtained sepia-colored powder was stored in the dark, until use. Following the above steps, approximately 30 mg of CNDs were synthesized.
Information regarding the calculation of QY is given in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and selectivity study of the CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: For the stability study of CNDs, the PL intensity of their aqueous solutions at a concentration of 60 μg mL−1 in the pH range of 1.0–12.0 was recorded at 450 nm, following excitation at 350 nm, after 30 s. Similarly, the stability was examined using solutions with different ionic strength (0–1.0 M) using sodium chloride and sodium sulfate. With regard to the selectivity study for the pH sensing, 60 μg mL−1 of CNDs were added to aqueous solutions with pH 6.0, 7.0, or 8.0, and a potentially interfering compound or metal ion was added. After stirring for 30 s, the PL intensity was recorded at 450 nm after excitation at 350 nm. The PL intensity was compared with the control sample (CND solution in the absence of the interfering compound or ion).

A water sample was adjusted to pH 11.0 and N,P-CNDs were added (final concentration: 60 μg mL−1). After vortex stirring for 30 sec, the PL intensity was recorded at 450 nm, following excitation at 350 nm. A sample from the effluent of the municipal treatment plant of Ioannina (Greece) was collected. After filtration to remove particulate matter, the pH of the sample was adjusted to 11.0 with NaOH (0.1 M) and was analyzed using the developed fluorescent probe.

In order to examine whether the N,P-CNDs could be used to detect the activity of urease, to a solution (pH 7.0) containing 50 μM urea, N,P-CNDs were added and the PL intensity was recorded at 450 nm after excitation at 350 nm. Then, 100 U L-1 urease was added and after incubating the mixture at 37 °C for 10 min the PL was recorded again. The pH shift was calculated using the equation: y = -19x + 256, where y denotes the PL intensity after the addition of urease and × the pH value of the solution, after incubation.

The HaCaT cells were cultured in DMEM medium containing 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum, 1 % antibiotics, and 1 % L-glutamine and were grown in a humidified 5% carbon dioxide atmosphere, at 37 °C and 5% CO2 [33].

HaCaT cells were seeded in 96-well plates and the cell viability assay was carried out according to our previous study [34]. The N,P-CNDs were examined at the concentrations of 100, 200, 400, 600, and 800 μg mL−1, after incubation with the cells for 24 h. After staining with crystal violet solution, cells were destained with methanol and the absorbance was measured using a UV–vis spectrometer.

HaCaT cells were seeded on 22 × 22 mm sterile glass coverslips, which were placed inside a 6-well plate and allowed to adhere overnight, under the conditions described in Section 2.8. The medium was replaced with a fresh one containing 200 μg mL−1 of N,P-CNDs, and the cells were incubated for 5 h. Then, they were washed with phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (three times) to remove the excess of N,P-CNDs, and PBS was added, previously adjusted to pH 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, and 7.0. After 30 min of incubation at 37 °C in the absence of CO2, the cells were examined with a fluorescence microscope under UV light. Images were captured for each sample and the fluorescence intensity was measured using the Image J software.

To ensure that the optimum N,P-CNDs were obtained from the synthesis, the conditions of the synthesis were optimized. To this end, the volume ratio of phosphoric acid – to – 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone, as well as the intensity and time of microwave irradiation were optimized. To select the optimum material, the maximum PL intensity and the quenching percentage caused by BP1 were used as criteria. From our results (Fig. S1), it was concluded that all N,P-CNDs synthesized from the microwave treatment of phosphoric acid and 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone mixtures of different volume ratios exhibited similar fluorescence spectra (maximum emission at 450–460 nm for excitation at 350–360 nm). Maximum PL intensity (and a QY of 53.8%) was obtained when phosphoric acid and 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone were mixed in a 1:1 v/v ratio, although the CNDs exhibited almost the same % PL quenching in all other cases (data not shown) accompanied by a ∼ 1% decrease in the QY. Moreover, all CNDs prepared from all different ratios had pH-sensing properties (vide infra). The pH sensing properties of the CNDs prepared using 3:1, 1:1, and 1:3 v/v ratio (phosphoric acid : 1-methyl-2-pyrrolidone) were similar, while a decrease (5–10%) in the sensitivity of the CNDs prepared with the 5:1 and 1:5 ratios were observed. Based on all the above, the 1:1 ratio was selected as the optimum.

The microwave irradiation intensity and time were also examined to further optimize the synthesized material. It was observed that when microwave intensity was ≥ 400 Watt, almost no fluorescent N,P-CNDs was obtained (accompanied by a sharp decrease in the QY reaching < 4%). Therefore, an intensity of 350 Watt was used for the synthesis even though lower intensity could be used at longer irradiation times. With regard to the irradiation time, mixtures were irradiated for 1, 2, and 3 min. A 2-min irradiation yielded N,P-CNDs with almost twice as high PL intensity and QY as that of 1-min irradiation (data not shown), whereas at a 3-min irradiation time a significant decline (15–20%) in the PL intensity was recorded (QY: 41.8%). The QY of the CNDs resulting from the optimum conditions (i.e. 53.8%) was found comparable or even better than that of other previously reported N,P-CNDs [14,35]. For instance, Sun et al. reported the synthesis of N,P-CNDs via microwave treatment (800 W for 40 s) of m-phenylenediamine, ortho-phosphoric acid and ethylenediamine and the QY of the CNDs was found to be 51% [35]. Zuo et al. reported the mixing of arginine, acetic acid, phosphorus pentoxide and 1,2-ethylenediamine that resulted in the formation of N,P-CNDs (based on the heat emitted by the acid-base neutralization) with a QY of 8.7% [14]. By optimizing the above parameters, the synthesis of N,P-CNDs with high PL intensity, superior QY, and desired fluorescence characteristics was achieved.

The synthesized N,P-CNDs were characterized to provide valuable insights into their structural and compositional properties. The FTIR spectrum of the synthesized N,P-CNDs can be seen in Fig. 1 . As can be seen, there is a broad band around 3335 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H groups [11]. The peaks at 1655 cm−1 and 1090 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of C = C and NH2, and bending vibrations of P = O groups, respectively [9,16]. The peak at 875 cm−1 can be ascribed to C-H bending [36]. The TEM image of the CNDs and their size distribution can be seen in Fig. 2 . It is apparent that the synthesized CNDs have an average size of 4 ± 1 nm. Fig. S2 depicts the survey XPS spectrum of the CNDs. In the sample, carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) are detected. Fig. 3 A shows the deconvoluted C1s XPS peak. The peak consists of 5 components: C-C with sp2 and sp3 configuration, hydroxyls or epoxides (C-O(H)), carbonyls (C = O) and carboxyls (O = C-OH) [37]. Fig. 3B shows the deconvoluted N1s XPS peak. The peak consists of 2 components at binding energies 400 ± 0.1 eV (N1) and 401.6 ± 0.1 eV (N2) associated with C-NH-C = O bonds and to positively charged nitrogen atoms (–NH3 +) [38]. Fig. 3C shows the P2p XPS doublet with a spin–orbit splitting of 0.9 eV. The P2p3/2 centered at 133.5 ± 0.1 eV is assigned to P-O bonds. More details about the % components concentration are given in Supplementary material, along with the % atomic concentration. An XRD analysis (Fig. S3) was carried out to assess the crystallinity of CNDs. A broad peak at around 2θ = 22° can be seen which indicates the amorphous nature of dots [39,40]. Finally, the UV–Vis spectrum of the CNDs is given in Fig. S4.

The emission spectra of the N,P-CNDs under various excitation wavelengths are presented in Fig. 4 . As can be seen, the CNDs exhibit a maximum emission wavelength at 450 nm after excitation at 350 nm. When they were excited at higher wavelengths, the emission maximum decreased and a redshift (up to 30 nm) was also observed. The excitation spectrum of the N,P-CNDs at an emission of 450 nm is presented in Fig. S5. All experiments discussed from this point on were performed at an excitation of 350 nm and the PL intensity was recorded at 450 nm.

First, the PL intensity of N,P-CNDs in aqueous solutions with different pH (i.e., 1.0–12.0) was examined. The results in Fig. S6 show that the PL intensity decreases in a linear sharp manner between pH 3.0 and 12.0. Next, the PL intensity of N,P-CNDs in solutions with different ionic strength was evaluated. As can be seen in Fig. S7, the PL intensity of the N,P-CNDs remains unaltered in solutions of high ionic strength. Finally, the resistance of CNDs to photobleaching was examined. To this end, a N,P-CNDs solution was irradiated for 3 h with a 150 W xenon lamp. The PL intensity remained almost unaltered (<1% decrease of the PL intensity), bespeaking good photostability. Finally, a CNDs solution was found to exhibit stable PL intensity after 30 days of storage, at 4 °C.

To evaluate the suitability of the developed N,P-CNDs for cell bioimaging, HaCaT cells were selected as model cell line because they are a commonly used keratinocyte cell line that has been widely used in biomedical research [33]. HaCaT cells have been shown to be useful in studying various aspects of skin biology and have been used extensively in the study of wound healing. Given that pH alterations play a significant role in wound healing progression and tumor diagnosis, HaCaT cells serve as an appropriate model for this study This is due to the pH sensing properties of the synthesized N,P-CNDs, that will be discussed in section 3.6. [41]. Initially, the cell viability assessment (Fig. S10) showed that the viability of HaCaT cells was above 95% at concentrations up to 400 μg mL−1 and above 90% for up to 600 μg mL−1, after 24 h of incubation. These results indicate low toxicity of the synthesized N,P-CNDs towards cultured cells, under the experimental conditions.

For an effective fluorescent imaging, it is crucial to employ a probe that can specifically target the desired cell, organelle, or molecule of interest. The applicability of the synthesized N,P-CNDs as bioimaging agents was examined using HaCaT cells. As can be seen from Fig. S9, cells after treatment with CNDs exhibit strong fluorescence, under UV irradiation. Moreover, it can be seen that the cell nucleus is stained more intensely, compared with the rest of the cytosol. This is of high importance since the synthesized N,P-CNDs can be used to monitor the nucleus of cells more easily than other N,P-CNDs that stain the whole cells [42,43]. Notably, this specificity was achieved without using nuclear-targeting ligands [43], thus simplifying the whole procedure. Also, this preference of the N,P-CNDs for targeting the cell nucleus holds potential in drug delivery and photodynamic therapy applications for cancer treatment [43]. The cell targeting properties of the N,P-CNDs may be attributed to their surface charge [44]. As will be discussed in Section 3.5, the CNDs are positively charged at pH below 8.0, which facilitated their internalization by cells. However, since the pH of the nucleus is ∼ 0.5 units higher than that of the cytosol, the CNDs acquire a negative charge, promoting the interaction with the nucleus. This was also the case in the study of Jung et al. [44], where the authors synthesized zwitterionic carbon dots, whose positive charge facilitated internalization and negative charge facilitated interaction with cell nucleus.

The pH-dependent PL emission of the N,P-CNDs was utilized to develop a PL pH sensor. As can be seen in Fig. 5 , the decrease of PL is in a linear relationship with the increase of pH in the range of 3.0–12.0. The regression equation is F = -19pH + 256 and the coefficient of determination (R 2) was found to be 0.993, bespeaking excellent linearity. No shift of the PL emission maximum was observed at any of N,P-CNDs, at various pH values.

Considering the complex cellular environment, the selectivity of the CNDs to hydrogen ions was assessed, to ensure that the CNDs can be used as an intracellular pH sensor. For this purpose, selectivity towards hydrogen ions was assessed in solutions containing representative metal ions and some bioactive molecules in the pH range of 6.0–8.0 (the physiological pH region for most cells). As shown in Fig. S10, physiologically ubiquitous metal ions, such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, and Mg2+, even at concentrations above their physiological levels, did not cause any obvious PL change to pH measurement. Similarly, heavy or transition metal ions, such as Hg2+ and Pb2+, had almost no adverse effect on fluorescence intensity. Furthermore, in the presence of various bioactive molecules the fluorescence intensity of the CNDs exhibited negligible changes at different pH values. These results reveal that CNDs exhibit high selectivity toward hydrogen ions over metal ions and bioactive molecules.

To evaluate the reversibility and stability of the pH response, the PL intensity of the CNDs was examined upon cycling the pH of the solution between 3.0 and 12.0. After cycling five times, the PL intensity at pH 12.0 and 3.0 decreased by less than 1% in both cases (Fig. S11). This demonstrates the highly reversible response of the probe to pH changes, making it suitable for real-time monitoring applications.

To illustrate the feasibility of the fluorescent pH sensor in real-life samples, the proposed pH sensor was employed to measure the pH of real samples, including tap water, and lake water. The results (Table 1 ) obtained from the present method are comparable to those obtained with a pH meter, demonstrating that this N,P-CND-fluorescent pH sensor-based offers an excellent and accurate method for the pH monitoring of real water samples. The relative standard deviation (RSD) for the intra- and inter-day precision (5 tap water samples analyzed on the same day and three samples on five consecutive days) were in the range of 1.8–2.1% and 1.9–2.4%, respectively, demonstrating good precision.

To gain a better understanding of the possible mechanism behind the pH-dependent fluorescence changes, additional size and zeta potential measurements were carried out. The average hydrodynamic diameter of the N,P-CNDs at pH = 4.0, 6.0, and 8.0 is 8 nm, 24 nm, and 46 nm, respectively. There is an obvious change in the size with the increase in pH, which indicates the fluorescence quenching of the as-prepared N,P-CNDs at high pH might be attributed to the aggregation of nanoparticles [45]. The zeta potentials of N,P-CNDs at pH = 4.0, 6.0 and 8.0 were found to be + 2.89 mV, +0.79 mV and −0.38 mV. The shift from positive to negative values as the pH increases from 4.0 to 8.0 is consistent with the pH-induced deprotonation of the surface functional group. The deprotonation may cause electrostatic charging to N,P-CNDs and therefore, affects the fluorescent properties of N,P-CNDs. Based on the above observations, it was concluded that the variation of fluorescent intensity of N,P-CNDs, under different pH should be attributed to the reversible protonation/deprotonation of N,P-CNDs in acidic and alkaline media [36].

Another possible application of the pH-sensing properties of the CNDs would be the real-time monitoring of pH changes in a system, which are closely related to many biological processes [46]. Developing sensitive pH-meters for intracellular applications would be of high diagnostic significance [47]. Reversibility of the pH response, applicability over a wide range of pH and high sensitivity are the main assets of the developed pH-sensing system. Accompanied by the low cytotoxicity and the strong PL, the as-synthesized N,P-CNDs have a high potential for intracellular pH sensing. To examine whether this is the case, cells pre-incubated with N,P-CNDs were placed in PBS at different pH values and were observed under a fluorescence microscope. Images of cells are provided in Fig. 6 . The PL intensity of cells in each image was quantified using Image J and a plot of the intracellular PL intensity vs the pH was prepared. As can be seen in Fig. 7 , the results exhibit good linearity and sensitivity (18% decrease of PL intensity for every pH unit change) alluding to the potential of using the N,P-CNDs for non-invasive intracellular pH sensing and such a plot can be used for detecting minor pH alterations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of urea/urease activity based on the pH response of N,P-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the CNDs can be extended over the pH measurement of samples, to measure the activity of certain enzymes and validate the presence of an enzyme in a sample. To demonstrate this concept, the activity of urease was investigated as a proof of concept. Urea is a molecule of high importance for living organisms, as fluctuations in its concentration result in hepatic and kidney malfunction and dehydration [48]. On the other hand, urease is an important enzyme that is produced by many microorganisms and many diagnostic tests were based on the detection of the enzyme [49]. For instance, urease-based tests have been developed to detect Helicobacter pylori, which causes peptic ulcers and gastritis, as well as hepatic encephalopathy and hyperammonemia [49,50]. As urease catalyzes the hydrolysis of urea into carbon monoxide and ammonia, monitoring the pH can be used to study the progress of the reaction. To prove the applicability in such enzymatic systems, urease was added to a sample solution with an initial pH of 7.0. The pH increased to 8.6 due to ammonia production, as evidenced by the decline in PL intensity of the CNDs. This validates our hypothesis and hence, the N,P-CNDs can be used either for the quantification of urea or the detection and assessment of the enzymatic activity of urease. This concept can further be extended for the monitoring of other enzymatic reactions that induce pH changes.

Capitalizing on their promising PL properties, CNDs were examined for their potential to be used for BP1 probing. Preliminary experiments conclusively demonstrated that the PL of the N,P-CNDs is readily quenched in the presence of BP1 (Figs. S12 and S13), while the observed quenching is selective for BP1 over other examined organic compounds and metal ions. As such, N,P-CNDs were used to develop a PL probe for the detection of BP1, and various parameters were examined.

The PL spectra of a CNDs solution after additions of various BP1 concentrations can be seen in Fig. S14. As the concentration of BP1 increases the PL intensity decreases. To develop the PL-based probe, the pH of the solution was examined in the pH range of 1–12. The PL quenching remains constant for pH values between 1.0 and 8.0; an increase is observed above pH 8.0, with the maximum being recorded at pH 11.0 (Fig. S15). This can be attributed, primarily, to the difference in the UV spectrum of BP1 at pH 8.0 and pH 11.0 (Fig. S16). The increase in the absorption of BP1 close to the excitation wavelength hints towards an inner filter effect (vide infra). Therefore, the pH value of 11.0 was selected for carrying out further experiments. Next, experiments were carried out using solutions containing 30, 60, 120, and 200 μg mL−1 of N,P-CNDs to assess its impact on PL quenching. No difference in the PL quenching was observed in any of the tested solutions. Further experiments were carried out using a concentration of 60 μg mL−1 so as low consumption of N,P-CNDs, and satisfactory PL intensity are reconciled. Finally, the BP1 was added to a N,P-CNDs solution and after vigorous stirring, its PL intensity was measured at different time intervals. No difference in the quenching of the PL was observed as time increased; therefore, 30 sec equilibration time before recording was selected for further experiments.

The Stern-Volmer plot was drawn and can be seen in Fig. 8 . The quenching of the emitted PL is linear in the range of 1.7–76 μM (coefficient of determination was 0.994) while concentrations of BP1 up to 175 μM can be detected. The limit of quantification (calculated as 10 times the signal-to-noise ratio) was 1.7 μM and the limit of detection was 0.6 μM (calculated as 3 times the signal-to-noise ratio). The repeatability of the method was assessed by analyzing five different samples (at a concentration twice as low as the limit of quantification of BP1) on the same day and three samples on three consecutive days, for the within-day and between-day precision, respectively. The relative standard deviations for intra-day and inter-day precision were 2.1% and3.3%, respectively. Finally, experiments were carried out analyzing effluent water samples from the municipal treatment plant of Ioannina (Greece), spiked with 5 μM and 17 μM BP1 (three and ten times the limit of quantification). The recoveries were 94% and 96% respectively, which were satisfactory and highlight the potential of the method to be applied for routine analysis.

To elucidate the mechanism of action, first, the Stern-Volmer plots (Fig. S17) of F 0/F as a function of BP1 concentration were prepared. As can be seen, as the temperature increased, the slope of the curve decreased. This hints toward a static quenching mechanism, which is associated with the formation of a ground-state complex between the CNDs and BP1 [10,11]. Therefore, the decrease in the slopes of the Stern-Volmer plots is indicative of the dissociation of weakly bound complexes. To further strengthen the notion of the static quenching mechanism, UV–Vis spectra of N,P-CNDs, BP1, and their mixture, as well as the theoretical sum of the two first spectra were recorded (Fig. S18). It can be seen that the absorbance of a mix of CNDs-BP1 is lower than the theoretical value, validating our findings.

Next, it was evaluated whether the inner filter effect mechanism might also applicable. As can be seen in Fig. S19, the absorption spectrum of BP1 overlaps with the excitation spectrum of N,P-CNDs. Therefore, the inner filter effect was applicable and could be used for analytical purposes [9,34,51]. However, the quenching constant from the static mechanism is increased, due to the inner filter effect. The observed PL was corrected according to the equations given in Supplementary material. After correction, the fitter linear Stern-Volmer plot was prepared (Fig. S20) and the Ksv was found to be 2.0 × 104 M−1, bespeaking strong binding.

The synthesized N,P-CNDs exhibit strong PL which was reaped in several applications. The obtained N,P-CNDs display pH-dependent behavior, and can be applied as emission fluorescent probe to non-invasively monitor pH in living cells and thus, serve as promising candidates for real-time tracking of the intracellular pH in a broad range, in the disease diagnosis, biosensing, biomedical and biological fields. Moreover, they can further be exploited to develop highly sensitive clinical tests for urease activity, as well as to develop other methods for the detection/quantification of other enzymes also, that can influence the pH of a solution. Also, the N,P-CNDs were used for the first time to develop a CNDs-based PL probe for BP1 showcasing rapid response, low limit of quantification, wide linear range, and good repeatability and sensitivity. Overall, the developed N,P-CNDs exhibit multiple favorable characteristics which can find a use for more than one application. Taking advantage of the multi-response to pH and benzophenone, as well as of urease activity assessment, bioimaging, and intracellular pH sensing properties, the N,P-CNDs synthesized herein have broadened the avenues for investigating other CNDs with multiple applications. The synthesized N,P-CNDs possess favorable characteristics, making them valuable in a variety of contexts. Their unique characteristics and ability to respond to pH changes and interact with benzophenone, combined with their suitability for bioimaging and intracellular pH sensing, inherently opens up a new avenue for further research in this framework.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors want to thank the Laboratory of Immunology and Laboratory of Zoology of the Biological Applications and Technology Department (University of Ioannina) for providing access to their facilities. Many thanks are also due to Dr. L. Sygellou and Prof. A. Avgeropoulos for obtaining XPS spectra and TEM images, respectively. Finally, thanks are also due to I. Chousidis for his assistance in obtaining the fluorescence microscopy images.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.146686.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Validation of a new, commercially available dry rapid urease test for the diagnosis of Helicobacter pylori infection in gastric biopsies

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. van Keeken, E. van Hattum, W.A. de Boer, Validation of a new, commercially available dry rapid urease test for the diagnosis of Helicobacter pylori infection in gastric biopsies, Neth. J. Med. 64 (2006) 329–333.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Correction of inner-filter effect in fluorescence excitation-emission matrix spectrometry using Raman scatter
